Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
East Asian Traditions

Xuanzang

c. 602 – 664

Yogācāra, storehouse consciousness, and the layered structure of mind.

Xuanzang’s philosophy of mind is best understood as a rigorously anti-substantialist, representationalist, therapeutic, and transformative theory of consciousness. Its central claim is not merely that “everything is mental” in a loose idealist sense, but that ordinary experience is structured by cognitive construction: what we take to be a world of independently existing objects confronting an independently existing self is, at the deepest level of analysis, a dependently generated system of cognitive appearances, karmic dispositions, affective self-grasping, and conceptual imputation. The goal is not theoretical skepticism for its own sake. The goal is the transformation of the very basis of cognition so that the subject-object structure that sustains ignorance, craving, and suffering is undone.

Xuanzang stands at a remarkable point in the global history of philosophy of mind. In East Asia, he is the decisive transmitter and systematizer of late Indian Yogācāra, especially the Dharmapāla-Nālandā line. In a broader comparative frame, he articulates, over a millennium before much of modern European philosophy, a sophisticated account of mental representation, subliminal cognition, self-construction, perceptual error, intentionality, reflexive awareness, affective bias, and the transformation of consciousness. The closest Western parallels are not any single thinker, but a composite: Aristotle’s faculty psychology, Kant’s account of mind-structured appearance, Berkeleyan idealism, Hume’s bundle theory of the self, Brentano-Husserl intentionality, Freud’s unconscious, and contemporary theories of self-modeling and cognitive construction. Yet Xuanzang’s system differs sharply from all of these because it is Buddhist through and through: there is no substantial soul, no creator God, no permanent transcendental ego, and no theoretical separation between philosophy of mind, ethics, meditation, and liberation.

1. Historical position

Xuanzang, 玄奘, usually dated 602–664 CE, was a Tang dynasty monk, translator, pilgrim, and scholastic philosopher. He became famous for a long journey to India, returned to Chang’an in 645 with Sanskrit texts, and became one of the most important translators in Chinese Buddhist history. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy describes him as “one of the most illustrious figures in the history of scholastic Chinese Buddhism,” noting his sixteen-year pilgrimage and his role in initiating the Chinese Faxiang, or Yogācāra/Consciousness-Only, school together with his chief disciple Kuiji.

Xuanzang’s decisive philosophical formation occurred at Nālandā, where he studied under Śīlabhadra and was initiated into the Yogācāra lineage. The same account reports that he also studied Sanskrit, Brahmanical philosophy, Buddhist logic, Mahāyāna exegesis, and Madhyamaka doctrines. This matters because Xuanzang was not simply a devotional pilgrim or translator; he was trained in the most technically advanced Buddhist scholastic environment of his time. His philosophy of mind must therefore be read as a late, highly systematized form of Indian Buddhist analysis, not merely as a Chinese religious doctrine.

The tradition Xuanzang transmitted was Yogācāra, one of the two major Indian Mahāyāna philosophical traditions alongside Madhyamaka. Yogācāra developed doctrines that are directly relevant to philosophy of mind: mind-only or cognition-only, the three natures, and the eightfold classification of consciousness, including the defiled mind and storehouse consciousness. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy explicitly characterizes Yogācāra as a major contributor to metaphysical, epistemological, and phenomenological studies of consciousness.

Xuanzang’s central philosophical text is the Cheng weishi lun 成唯識論, often reconstructed in Sanskrit as Vijñaptimātratāsiddhi and translated as Demonstration of Consciousness-Only, Establishment of Cognition-Only, or Discourse on the Realization of Consciousness-Only. It comments on Vasubandhu’s Thirty Verses on Consciousness-Only and draws on ten Indian commentaries, privileging Dharmapāla’s interpretation. BDK America describes the text as a commentary on Vasubandhu’s Triṃśikā based on ten Indian commentaries and as a basic text of the Faxiang/Hossō school in China and Japan. The IEP adds an important nuance: it is the only Xuanzang translation that is not a direct translation of a single text but rather a selective and evaluative editorial work drawing on ten distinct sources.

That point is crucial. Xuanzang’s philosophy of mind is not “Xuanzang’s private invention,” but neither is it a passive translation. It is a Chinese scholastic synthesis of late Indian Yogācāra, especially Dharmapāla’s line, organized into a coherent doctrinal system. Modern scholarship continues to debate the Cheng weishi lun, especially the status of external objects in its argument, but it is widely treated as a major synthesis of Yogācāra thought.

2. The philosophical problem Xuanzang inherits

Xuanzang inherits several problems from earlier Buddhism and sharpens them into a philosophy of mind.

The first problem is continuity without a self. Buddhism rejects ātman: a permanent, independent, substantial self. Yet persons seem to remember, act, suffer consequences, develop habits, and undergo moral transformation. If there is no enduring self, what carries karmic traces? What explains memory, character, rebirth, and the continuity of agency?

The second problem is perception without naïve realism. Ordinary people take themselves to perceive external objects directly. Yogācāra argues that perception is structured by mental appearance. What appears as an object is already cognitively mediated. The problem is not simply whether an external world exists, but whether what appears as external, independent, stable, and self-subsistent can be established as such.

The third problem is selfhood as error. If there is no real self, why does self-experience feel so primitive and inescapable? Xuanzang’s answer is that self-experience is generated by a specific stratum of consciousness, the seventh consciousness, which appropriates the eighth consciousness as “I” or “mine.”

The fourth problem is liberation. If delusion is not merely a false belief but a deeply embodied, affective, karmically conditioned cognitive structure, then liberation cannot be mere assent to a doctrine. It must be a transformation of the architecture of mind.

This is why Xuanzang’s system is a philosophy of mind in a strong sense: it offers an account of mental architecture, representation, intentionality, selfhood, unconscious dispositions, affect, action, error, intersubjectivity, and cognitive transformation.

3. Basic terms: citta, manas, vijñāna, vijñapti

Buddhist philosophy does not begin with the modern Western assumption that “mind” is one simple thing. It distinguishes several overlapping terms.

Citta often means mind or thought. Manas can mean mental faculty or mentation. Vijñāna is consciousness or discriminative cognition. Vijñapti is cognitive presentation, representation, disclosure, or manifestation. The Stanford entry on Indian Buddhist philosophy notes that manas, vijñāna, and citta are sometimes used synonymously, but in Asaṅga’s Mahāyānasaṃgraha they are differentiated: citta refers to the Yogācāra receptacle consciousness, ālayavijñāna; manas refers to the mental domain and its afflictive tendencies; and vijñāna refers to the six types of cognitive awareness.

This triadic distinction is foundational for Xuanzang. Mind is not a single Cartesian res cogitans, not a unified immaterial substance. It is a layered process consisting of manifest cognitions, affective self-grasping, and subliminal dispositional storage.

The term vijñaptimātra is often translated “consciousness-only,” but “cognition-only,” “representation-only,” or “presentation-only” often better captures the epistemic and phenomenological force. The point is that what we encounter are cognitive presentations. Yogācāra’s signature doctrine gives mind or cognition priority over the sensible world, claiming that the sensible world depends for its nature and existence on being cognized by mind.

4. The architecture of mind: the eight consciousnesses

Classical Abhidharma generally analyzes consciousness into six types: five sensory consciousnesses and one mental consciousness. Yogācāra expands this to eight. The six ordinary consciousnesses are supraliminal and intermittent; the seventh and eighth operate subliminally and continuously. Stanford’s Yogācāra entry explains that classical Yogācāra distinguishes eight types of consciousness: the five sense consciousnesses, mental consciousness, the defiled or afflicted mind, and the storehouse or substratum consciousness.

The first five consciousnesses are sensory: visual, auditory, olfactory, gustatory, and tactile. They are not “faculties” in the modern generic sense, but momentary cognitive events corresponding to sensory domains. They do not yet constitute the full world of ordinary experience; they are coordinated with other layers of consciousness.

The sixth consciousness, manovijñāna, is mental consciousness. It synthesizes sensory data, judges, imagines, remembers, compares, reasons, dreams, and conceptualizes. It is the main site of discursive thought. It is also the site where coarse philosophical and religious errors arise: false views about self, dharmas, substances, external objects, and metaphysical categories.

The seventh consciousness, kliṣṭamanas, is the afflicted or defiled mind. Its function is self-appropriation. It takes the eighth consciousness as its object and misconstrues it as a real self. Yogācāra describes it as marked by four defilements: false view of self, delusion about self, self-conceit, and self-love. This is one of Xuanzang’s most important contributions to philosophy of mind: the self is not merely a conceptual mistake at the surface of cognition; it is generated by a persistent, affectively charged, pre-reflective layer of appropriation.

The eighth consciousness, ālayavijñāna, is storehouse, repository, or substratum consciousness. It stores karmic seeds, sustains continuity, and conditions the arising of manifest cognitions. It functions even when ordinary consciousness ceases, as in deep meditative states, and it helps explain life-continuity, rebirth, and the reactivation of consciousness after interruption. It is the most philosophically delicate element of the system because it performs some explanatory roles that other Indian systems assign to a self, but Yogācāra insists it is not a self. It is compared to a river: continuous in flow, but constituted by changing, impermanent events.

The basic model is therefore stratified:

Ordinary experience is generated by manifest sensory and mental cognitions.

These cognitions are permeated by the seventh consciousness’s egoic appropriation.

They are causally conditioned by seeds stored in the eighth consciousness.

The eighth consciousness is itself conditioned, impermanent, morally neutral in its basic function, and transformable.

This is a dynamic systems model of mind, not a substance model.

5. Mind and mental factors

Xuanzang’s system does not treat consciousness as bare awareness plus external content. Each moment of cognition is accompanied by mental factors, caitasikas, which shape its affective, attentional, volitional, and ethical character. In Yogācāra taxonomy, there are “mind kings” or primary consciousnesses and “mental factors” that arise with and modify them. A traditional exposition of Xuanzang’s eight-consciousness teaching states that the first five consciousnesses interact with a range of mental factors such as attention, contact, feeling, conceptualization, deliberation, desire, resolution, recollection, concentration, judgment, wholesome states, and afflictive states.

The sixth consciousness is especially important because it can interact with all fifty-one mental factors. This has major philosophical consequences. Perception is not a neutral photographic reception of data. Experience is always already colored by attention, feeling-tone, conceptualization, volition, memory, desire, anger, ignorance, faith, shame, diligence, concentration, and wisdom. The mind is not just representational; it is affectively and ethically structured.

This allows Xuanzang to integrate philosophy of mind with moral psychology. What one sees, and how one sees, depends on the seeds and mental factors active in cognition. The distinction between epistemic error and moral-affective distortion is therefore not sharp. Ignorance is not merely lack of information. It is a mode of cognition structured by self-grasping and karmically conditioned affect.

6. Seeds, perfuming, and dispositional mind

The theory of seeds, bīja, is the engine of Xuanzang’s account of continuity and transformation. A seed is a latent disposition stored in the ālayavijñāna. Actions, perceptions, emotions, and thoughts “perfume” the storehouse consciousness, leaving traces that later ripen into further experiences, tendencies, and actions.

This provides a Buddhist account of habit, character, memory, affective conditioning, and karmic continuity without invoking a permanent self. The eighth consciousness holds seeds of virtuous, non-virtuous, and neutral mental events, allowing dissimilar moments of consciousness to arise in a coherent causal stream. Stanford’s Yogācāra entry notes that one argument for ālayavijñāna is precisely that ordinary consciousnesses with different moral qualities cannot adequately condition one another directly; a morally neutral storehouse is needed to hold the seeds of all of them.

In modern terms, this resembles a dispositional or subpersonal model of mind. But the comparison must be handled carefully. Seeds are not genes, not memories in the ordinary sense, not unconscious wishes in the Freudian sense, and not neural representations. They are karmic potentials in a Buddhist causal framework. Still, as an abstract theory of mind, the seed model is strikingly sophisticated: present cognition is shaped by latent traces of past cognition, and present action modifies the latent conditions of future cognition.

7. The self as constructed misrecognition

Xuanzang’s analysis of selfhood is one of the most important parts of the system for philosophy of mind.

The self is not simply absent. It is actively produced. The seventh consciousness continuously takes the eighth consciousness as its object and mistakes the flowing storehouse stream for an enduring subject. This means that the “I” is a cognitive-affective misrecognition: a process takes another process as a substance.

This distinguishes Xuanzang from simple bundle theories. A bundle theory may say that the self is nothing over and above a collection of perceptions. Xuanzang says more: the mind contains a mechanism that appropriates the stream and generates the appearance of an owner, thinker, feeler, and agent. The self is therefore a dependently arisen illusion, not a mere nothing.

The error is both cognitive and affective. The seventh consciousness does not merely form the proposition “I exist.” It clings. It loves itself, conceives itself, defends itself, and organizes experience around itself. This is why philosophical insight alone is insufficient. One must transform the dispositions that generate self-grasping.

This gives Xuanzang a powerful account of egocentric bias. Ordinary cognition is not merely mistaken about objects; it is organized around an implicit self-model. The “world” appears as a field of things relevant to “me.” This is structurally close to some contemporary accounts of the self as a model or construct, but Xuanzang embeds the idea in a soteriological and karmic framework.

8. Subject-object duality

For Xuanzang, the ordinary mind divides experience into grasper and grasped: subject and object. This duality is the basic form of ignorance. The grasper corresponds to attachment to self; the grasped corresponds to attachment to dharmas, or supposedly real objects/categories. A traditional exposition of Consciousness-Only states that the basic obstacles arise from consciousness dividing the world into grasper and grasped, and that these correspond to attachment to self and attachment to dharmas.

This is not a denial that there is experience. It is a denial that experience ultimately has the dualistic structure it appears to have. Ordinary cognition presents itself as “I, here, apprehend that object, there.” Yogācāra says this structure is constructed. The object-side and subject-side are both appearances within cognition.

This is where Xuanzang differs from both naïve realism and Cartesian internalism. Against naïve realism, he denies that cognition simply opens onto external things as they are. Against Cartesianism, he denies that the subject-pole is an indubitable mental substance. Both object and subject are dependently arisen structures within cognitive manifestation.

9. The three natures

The three natures, trisvabhāva, provide Xuanzang’s most important ontological and phenomenological framework.

The imagined nature, parikalpita-svabhāva, is the falsely constructed world of independent subjects and objects. It is how things appear to ordinary, unenlightened cognition. It is especially tied to language and conceptual imputation: we mistake our categories for the structure of reality.

The dependent nature, paratantra-svabhāva, is the causal flow of cognition and conditions. It is not false in the way the imagined nature is false, but it is not ultimately grasped correctly so long as it is overlaid with subject-object duality.

The perfected nature, pariniṣpanna-svabhāva, is the dependent nature seen free from imagined duality. It is the absence of the imagined nature in the dependent nature. Stanford summarizes this by saying that the perfected nature is grasped when one realizes that imagined entities such as subjects and objects are unreal products of conceptualizing activity.

This is one of the most elegant parts of the system. Xuanzang does not say that ordinary experience is sheer nothing. Nor does he say that the ordinary world is ultimately real as it appears. Instead:

The world as imagined is false.

The causal flow that gives rise to that world is dependently real.

The ultimate truth is that this causal flow is empty of the dualities projected onto it.

Thus, “consciousness-only” is not merely metaphysical idealism. It is an analysis of how false objectivity and false subjectivity arise within dependent cognition.

10. Perception and the critique of external objects

Yogācāra’s critique of external objects develops partly from earlier Buddhist representationalism. The Sautrāntikas argued that one is immediately aware only of mental representations and can only infer external objects. Yogācāra radicalizes this: if external objects are only inferred, and if experience can be explained through mental causes, why posit mind-independent objects at all? Stanford notes that Sautrāntika representationalism helped catalyze Yogācāra idealism, especially by challenging direct realism.

One Yogācāra argument turns on time-lag and momentariness. If cognition takes time, then by the moment one judges “this object is perceived by me,” both the visual consciousness and the visible object, assuming Buddhist momentariness, have already ceased. The alleged mind-independent object known by direct perception is therefore not actually available as direct perceptual support.

Another argument appeals to dreams, hallucinations, and shared delusive experiences. Vasubandhu’s Twenty Verses argues that the realist’s criteria for external objects—spatiotemporal order, intersubjective agreement, and practical efficacy—can be matched by cases where no external object is present, such as dreams or shared hell-visions. The point is not that waking life is exactly a dream, but that the realist has not shown that external objects are necessary to explain the structure of experience.

Xuanzang’s version of Consciousness-Only is thus a form of explanatory economy: the appearances, regularities, and practical effects of experience can be explained through consciousness, seeds, shared karmic conditioning, and dependent cognition. The external object, conceived as an independent material cause of perception, is not established.

Yet this does not entail simple solipsism. Yogācāra recognizes a serious problem of other minds, and Stanford notes that solipsism would be unwelcome because it conflicts with the Mahāyāna aim of liberating all sentient beings. Intersubjectivity is explained through shared or common seeds resulting from shared actions and experiences over beginningless time.

11. Reflexive awareness and the four aspects of cognition

Xuanzang’s Yogācāra also contains a sophisticated theory of reflexive awareness. In Indian Buddhist epistemology, Dignāga and Dharmakīrti argue that cognition is self-revealing or reflexively aware. A cognition does not merely reveal an object; it also reveals itself. Stanford’s entry on Indian Buddhist philosophy describes this as a dual-aspect theory: cognition has a subjective appearance and an objective appearance, and reflexive awareness explains why cognition does not occur “in the dark.”

The Dharmapāla-Xuanzang line develops this further through the four aspects or four parts of cognition:

The image-aspect or object-aspect: what appears as the object.

The seeing-aspect or subject-aspect: the apprehending side.

Self-cognition: the cognition’s awareness of itself.

Awareness of self-cognition: a higher-order confirmation that prevents regress.

The point of this structure is to explain how cognition can be both object-directed and self-intimating without requiring a separate soul-substance. A cognition is not a hidden thing that needs another cognition to illuminate it from outside. Its appearing includes its self-presentation.

This is one of the places where Xuanzang most strongly converges with later Western discussions of intentionality and consciousness. Brentano famously defined intentionality as the mind’s directedness toward an object, including objects that may exist only “in” the mind. Yogācāra had already analyzed cognition as object-directed while also questioning whether the object exists independently of cognition.

12. The body, embodiment, and life-continuity

Xuanzang is not a Cartesian dualist. The body is not an independently existing material machine animated by a separate mind-substance. But neither is the body irrelevant. The ālayavijñāna supports life, takes possession of a new existence, maintains the body, and withdraws at death. Stanford notes that Yogācāra texts assign the ālayavijñāna a biological role: it takes possession of a new life at conception, keeps the body alive, and withdraws at death.

This means the mind-body relation in Xuanzang is neither modern physicalism nor substance dualism. The body is part of the dependently arisen field of consciousness; it is also a condition for ordinary cognition. The five sensory consciousnesses depend on faculties, objects, attention, seeds, and deeper bases of consciousness. A traditional explanation of Xuanzang’s eight-consciousness verses says that the first five consciousnesses depend on multiple preconditions, including sense faculty, object, attention, the sixth consciousness, the seventh consciousness, the eighth consciousness, and seeds.

Thus Xuanzang’s view is embodied but not materialist. The body is integrated into the cognitive process, but “body” itself is not a mind-independent substance known as it is in itself.

13. The unconscious: ālaya and Freud

The ālayavijñāna is often compared to the unconscious, and the comparison is useful if handled carefully. Like the unconscious, it operates beneath ordinary awareness and shapes conscious life. It stores dispositions, drives patterns of perception and action, and explains why conscious life is conditioned by forces not transparent to introspection.

But it is not Freud’s unconscious. Freud’s unconscious is structured around repression, instinctual drives, neurotic symptoms, and psychoanalytic recovery. The IEP describes Freud’s unconscious as mental processes that cannot be brought to consciousness except through extended psychoanalysis, and as something that shows the mind cannot be identified with consciousness. Xuanzang’s ālaya is broader and more metaphysical: it is the repository of karmic seeds, the support of continuity, and the causal basis for the arising of manifest consciousness.

The best comparison is this: Freud and Xuanzang both reject the equation of mind with transparent consciousness. But Freud’s unconscious is primarily psychodynamic; Xuanzang’s storehouse consciousness is karmic, cognitive, dispositional, and soteriological.

14. Error, language, and conceptual construction

For Xuanzang, language and conceptuality are central to illusion. The imagined nature arises when we take names, categories, and conceptual distinctions as corresponding to intrinsically existing things. We divide experience into subject, object, property, relation, self, other, body, world, and then forget that these are conceptual constructions imposed on dependent arising.

This does not mean all conceptual thought is useless. Xuanzang’s own system is highly conceptual. The point is that concepts are therapeutic tools when used correctly and fetters when reified. Buddhist philosophy uses conceptual analysis to undo conceptual reification.

This is a subtle position. It is neither anti-intellectualism nor rationalism. It is a disciplined use of reason against the absolutization of reason’s own products.

15. Ethics and agency without a self

If there is no self, why are persons responsible? Xuanzang answers through causal continuity. Intentional actions leave seeds. Seeds ripen into future experiences and dispositions. Responsibility does not require an eternal subject; it requires a causally continuous stream in which intentions condition future suffering or liberation.

Volition, cetanā, is a mental factor. It directs the mind and generates karmic consequences. Agency is therefore not the action of a metaphysical ego but the operation of intention within a causal stream. This resembles some contemporary compatibilist and process-based accounts of agency, but Xuanzang’s version is ethical and soteriological from the start.

The theory also explains moral transformation. Since present actions perfume the storehouse, practice can weaken unwholesome seeds and strengthen wholesome ones. Meditation, ethical restraint, study, and insight are not external additions to mind; they are interventions in the causal structure of cognition.

16. Liberation as transformation of the basis

The final aim is āśraya-parāvṛtti, transformation or revolution of the basis. This means the transformation of the deep structures that generate delusive cognition. Liberation is not merely having true beliefs. It is the reorganization of the entire cognitive-affective-dispositional system.

The path involves learning, reasoning, and cultivation. Modern scholarship emphasizes that Xuanzang’s Discourse is structured as a path leading progressively to awakening, and that Yogācāra treats understanding cognitive functions as a way to attain Buddhahood.

A traditional account of the Consciousness-Only path describes a five-stage process: resources, application, vision, meditational development, and perfection. It states that the transformation of consciousness into wisdom begins with the path of vision, where true suchness is first directly experienced.

In later Yogācāra and East Asian formulations, the eight consciousnesses are transformed into four wisdoms:

The first five consciousnesses become the wisdom of successful performance.

The sixth consciousness becomes the wisdom of wonderful observation.

The seventh consciousness becomes the wisdom of equality.

The eighth consciousness becomes great mirror wisdom.

A traditional explanation says that by the eighth bodhisattva ground the outflowing activity of the sixth and seventh consciousnesses is permanently ended, while great mirror wisdom and successful performance wisdom fully function at Buddhahood.

The philosophical point is profound: mind is not eliminated; it is purified of dualistic distortion. Consciousness as ignorance becomes wisdom as non-dual cognition.

17. Is Xuanzang an idealist?

The answer depends on what “idealism” means.

If idealism means that the objects of ordinary experience are mind-dependent appearances, then Xuanzang is an idealist.

If idealism means that the causes of perception are mental seeds rather than external material objects, Xuanzang is again close to idealism.

If idealism means that ultimate reality is a mental substance, a cosmic mind, or an absolute subject, Xuanzang is not straightforwardly an idealist.

Stanford’s Yogācāra entry distinguishes epistemological, phenomenological, and ontological readings of Yogācāra. It notes that some interpreters compare Yogācāra to Kantian transcendental idealism or Husserlian phenomenology, while others read it as ontological idealism that rejects anything beyond minds and mental states. It also warns that Yogācāra differs from Berkeley’s perceiving souls and Hegel’s absolute spirit because it rejects a substantial self and rejects subject-object duality at the ultimate level.

The most careful formulation is this: Xuanzang is a cognition-only philosopher for whom ordinary objects and subjects are dependently arisen cognitive constructions; he is not a subjective idealist who reduces the universe to my personal mind, nor a Berkeleyan immaterialist grounded in God, nor a German idealist of absolute spirit.

18. Comparison with Western philosophy of mind

Aristotle

Aristotle’s psychology treats soul as the form or actuality of a living body. This avoids crude dualism and crude materialism. Stanford notes that Aristotle’s hylomorphism steers a middle course between reductive materialism and Platonic dualism, treating the soul as the actuality of the body rather than as a separable substance in the ordinary case.

Xuanzang and Aristotle converge in rejecting a simplistic separation of mind and body. Both analyze mental life through functions and capacities. Both treat perception as structured rather than as passive reception. But they differ fundamentally. Aristotle’s soul is the form of a living organism; Xuanzang’s mind is a stream of momentary cognitions conditioned by karmic seeds. Aristotle’s psychology is biological and teleological; Xuanzang’s is karmic, phenomenological, and soteriological. Aristotle does not deny the independent reality of the external world in the Yogācāra sense, nor does he analyze selfhood as an afflictive misappropriation of a subliminal consciousness.

Stoicism

Stoic psychology is relevant because it treats impressions, assent, and impulse as central mental functions. Stanford notes that the Stoics distinguish ensouled life by representational and motivational faculties, especially impression and impulse, and give rational creatures the faculty of assent.

Xuanzang converges with Stoicism in making cognition ethically charged. We are not merely passive receivers of appearances; how we take appearances matters. But Stoicism is corporealist: the soul is a subtle body or pneuma. Xuanzang’s system is not corporealist. More importantly, Stoicism emphasizes rational assent as an ethical act, whereas Xuanzang emphasizes deep subliminal seeds and self-grasping processes that precede ordinary rational judgment.

Descartes

Descartes is the canonical Western starting point for the modern mind-body problem. Stanford summarizes his view: mind and body are distinct substances, characterized by thought and extension respectively, and this theory became a standard starting point for later mind-body debates.

Xuanzang is almost anti-Cartesian in advance. He does not treat mind as a thinking substance. He does not ground certainty in an indubitable ego. He does not sharply separate mental substance from extended substance. He would regard the Cartesian “I think” as still infected by the seventh consciousness’s appropriation. For Xuanzang, the thinking “I” is precisely what must be analyzed and deconstructed.

Yet there is a convergence: both Descartes and Xuanzang begin from the problem of appearance. Dreams, illusions, and misperceptions matter. Both ask how experience can present a world that may not be as it appears. But Descartes moves toward the certainty of the thinking subject; Xuanzang moves toward the emptiness of both subject and object.

Berkeley

Berkeley’s immaterialism is the obvious Western analogue to “mind-only.” Stanford describes Berkeley’s immaterialism as the modern paradigm of metaphysical idealism: all that exists are ideas and the minds, finite or divine, that have them.

Xuanzang is similar insofar as external matter is not established as an independent basis of experience. But the differences are decisive. Berkeley keeps substantial minds and God. Xuanzang rejects substantial selfhood and does not appeal to a divine perceiver. Berkeley’s idealism preserves a stable theistic metaphysics; Xuanzang’s Consciousness-Only is embedded in dependent arising, no-self, karmic causation, and meditative transformation.

Hume

Hume’s bundle theory is closer to Buddhism. Stanford’s dualism entry notes that Hume replaced the Cartesian mind with a bundle of fleeting perceptions in a way reminiscent of, and possibly influenced by, earlier Buddhist theories.

Xuanzang and Hume converge in rejecting the substantial self. But Xuanzang’s account is more architecturally complex. He does not merely say that introspection finds only perceptions. He explains why the mind falsely constructs a self: the seventh consciousness appropriates the eighth. He also explains continuity through seeds and storehouse consciousness, whereas Hume famously struggles with personal identity and causal connection.

Kant

Kant is perhaps the most illuminating comparison. Kant argues that space and time are forms of our sensible intuition, not properties of things in themselves, and that objects in space and time are appearances. This resembles Yogācāra in that the world as experienced is conditioned by cognitive structure.

The difference is equally important. Kant retains things-in-themselves, although unknowable in substantive respects. Xuanzang does not simply posit noumena behind appearances. His analysis centers on the dependent flow of cognition and the absence of imagined duality. Kant also retains a transcendental unity of apperception as a condition of experience; Xuanzang treats the deep sense of “I” as an afflictive construction to be overcome.

Kant asks: what must the mind contribute for experience of objects to be possible?

Xuanzang asks: what cognitive-affective structures generate the delusive appearance of subjects and objects, and how can they be transformed?

Brentano and Husserl

Brentano’s revival of intentionality defines mental phenomena through directedness toward an object. This converges strongly with Buddhist theories of cognition as object-directed. Stanford’s entry on consciousness and intentionality cites Brentano’s claim that every mental phenomenon includes something as object within itself.

Husserlian phenomenology also resembles Yogācāra because it brackets naïve assumptions about external reality and analyzes structures of appearance. Modern interpreters such as Dan Lusthaus have explicitly read Yogācāra as Buddhist phenomenology, though this reading is contested. The contemporary debate over whether Yogācāra is phenomenology, epistemological idealism, or ontological idealism remains active.

Xuanzang differs from phenomenology in adding karmic causality, meditative soteriology, and the storehouse consciousness. Husserl analyzes intentional structures; Xuanzang analyzes intentional structures plus the subliminal karmic conditions that generate them and the path that transforms them.

Freud and psychoanalysis

Freud and Xuanzang converge in rejecting the transparency of consciousness. Conscious life is not self-explanatory. Hidden structures shape what appears, what is desired, and how one acts. But Freud’s model is therapeutic in a clinical sense, centered on repression, symptom, libido, and psychoanalytic interpretation. Xuanzang’s model is therapeutic in a Buddhist sense, centered on ignorance, karmic seeds, self-grasping, meditation, and awakening.

Contemporary philosophy of mind

Xuanzang anticipates several contemporary themes, though not in modern scientific terms:

Perception is representational rather than direct.

The self is constructed.

Much cognition is subpersonal or subliminal.

Affective and attentional factors shape perception.

Embodied life and cognitive life are interdependent.

Consciousness has reflexive or self-presenting features.

Subject-object duality may be a feature of ordinary cognition rather than ultimate reality.

At the same time, Xuanzang is not a cognitive scientist before his time. His theory is embedded in karma, rebirth, bodhisattva practice, and Buddhist liberation. Modern analogies are useful only when they do not erase that context.

19. Main convergences and differences in compact form

Topic Xuanzang Closest Western parallel Decisive difference Mind-body Body and mind are dependently integrated through consciousness, faculties, seeds, and ālaya Aristotle’s hylomorphism Xuanzang is not biological hylomorphist; his account is karmic and cognition-only Self Self is a constructed misappropriation by the seventh consciousness Hume’s bundle theory, contemporary self-model theory Xuanzang explains the mechanism of self-grasping and ties it to liberation Perception Objects appear as cognitive constructions conditioned by seeds Representationalism, Kant, phenomenology Xuanzang denies ultimate subject-object duality and embeds perception in karma Unconscious Ālaya stores seeds and conditions experience Freud, depth psychology Ālaya is not repression or libido; it is karmic-dispositional continuity Idealism The sensible world is cognition-dependent Berkeley, Kant No God, no substantial mind, no transcendental ego Intentionality Cognition is object-directed but object and subject are internal aspects of cognition Brentano, Husserl Intentionality is to be transformed, not merely described Ethics Mental factors and seeds make cognition morally charged Stoic assent, virtue ethics Ethics is inseparable from karmic causation and awakening

20. Strengths of Xuanzang’s philosophy of mind

Xuanzang’s first strength is that he avoids both substance dualism and reductive materialism. Mind is not an immaterial thing; body is not an independently given material substrate; experience is a dependently arisen process.

His second strength is the depth of his account of selfhood. Many theories deny a substantial self. Xuanzang explains why selfhood nevertheless appears, why it is affectively powerful, and why it persists beneath ordinary reflection.

His third strength is the integration of cognition and affect. Perception is not neutral. It is shaped by attention, feeling, desire, aversion, ignorance, memory, and volition. This is philosophically and psychologically richer than models that isolate perception from emotion.

His fourth strength is the theory of dispositional continuity. Seeds allow Xuanzang to explain habit, character, memory, karmic result, and transformation without positing a permanent soul.

His fifth strength is that the theory is practical. It is not only about how the mind represents the world, but about how the mind can be trained, purified, and transformed.

21. Main difficulties and tensions

The first difficulty is the status of the external world. If Consciousness-Only denies external objects, how does it avoid solipsism? Yogācāra answers through multiple mind-streams and shared karmic seeds, but the problem remains philosophically demanding. Stanford notes that the idealism debate remains contested, with epistemological, phenomenological, and ontological readings all defended.

The second difficulty is the status of ālayavijñāna. It explains continuity, but because it performs some roles traditionally assigned to a self, critics accused Yogācāra of smuggling ātman back into Buddhism. Yogācāra replies that ālaya is impermanent, conditioned, and ceases with liberation. Still, the tension is real: the more explanatory work ālaya does, the more it risks looking like what Buddhism rejects.

The third difficulty is the epistemic status of seeds. Seeds explain too much too easily unless constrained. As a religious-philosophical theory, they are powerful; as an empirically testable psychological theory, they require translation and caution.

The fourth difficulty is the Faxiang doctrine of different spiritual capacities. The IEP notes that the school associated with Xuanzang and Kuiji argued that not all beings possess pure seeds and therefore not all are capable of attaining Buddhahood. This conflicts with more universalist Buddha-nature traditions and raises moral and soteriological concerns.

The fifth difficulty is whether the system over-intellectualizes liberation. Xuanzang’s system is extraordinarily analytic. Later Chinese traditions such as Chan and Huayan often criticized Faxiang for its scholastic complexity. The IEP notes that Faxiang declined after Xuanzang and Kuiji, in part because of criticism from Huayan and because its highly technical analysis was difficult for many Chinese Buddhists to accept.

22. A reconstructed Xuanzangian philosophy of mind

If we extract a comprehensive philosophy of mind from Xuanzang, it can be formulated as the following theses.

First, mind is not a substance but a stream of dependently arisen cognitive events and dispositions.

Second, consciousness is layered: manifest sensory and mental cognitions rest on subliminal self-grasping and a deeper storehouse of seeds.

Third, perception is not direct access to independent objects; it is cognition presenting itself in object-form.

Fourth, ordinary experience is structured by subject-object duality, but that duality is constructed, not ultimate.

Fifth, the self is not an entity but an affective-cognitive appropriation generated by the seventh consciousness.

Sixth, continuity does not require a soul; it requires causal transmission through seeds in the storehouse consciousness.

Seventh, unconscious or subliminal processes are indispensable for explaining conscious life.

Eighth, cognition is ethically charged because mental factors such as desire, aversion, ignorance, faith, shame, concentration, and wisdom shape every experience.

Ninth, language and concepts are necessary but dangerous: they help analyze experience, yet they also generate reification.

Tenth, reality must be analyzed through the three natures: imagined construction, dependent arising, and perfected non-duality.

Eleventh, intersubjectivity is possible because mind-streams are karmically coordinated through shared seeds and shared causal histories.

Twelfth, liberation is not the acquisition of a correct theory but the transformation of the basis of cognition.

Thirteenth, wisdom is transformed consciousness: the same mental continuum that produces delusion can, when purified, function as non-dual wisdom.

Fourteenth, philosophy of mind cannot be separated from ethics and contemplative practice.

Fifteenth, the deepest error is not believing in the wrong object, but inhabiting the wrong structure of experience: a world divided into self and object, grasper and grasped.

23. Final assessment

Xuanzang deserves a central place in any global history of philosophy of mind. His system is not a curiosity adjacent to “real” philosophy of mind; it is one of the most elaborate premodern theories of consciousness, representation, self-construction, unconscious conditioning, and cognitive transformation. It is more systematic than most ancient Western accounts of perception, more psychologically layered than early modern rationalism, less naïve about selfhood than Cartesianism, and more ethically integrated than much contemporary analytic philosophy of mind.

Its deepest contribution is the claim that the mind-body problem, the problem of perception, the problem of self, and the problem of suffering are not separate. They are different aspects of one delusive cognitive structure. To know the mind is to know how worlds are constructed, how selves are projected, how suffering is reproduced, and how cognition can be transformed into wisdom.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
